description: Participating in politics is a marathon not a sprint, requiring a multi-faceted approach to addressing community issues. Join us for RU Organizing: An Introduction to the Building Blocks of Organizing for Change on Tuesday, April 7th from 6:00PM to 8:30PM at the Eagleton Institute of Politics. Hosted by Dr. Janice Fine, Professor in the School of Management and Labor Relations and Director of Workplace Justice Lab@RU, attendees will develop skills of collective action and community organizing to systematically address issues at the campus, local, state, and federal level. Dinner will be served; open to current Rutgers University undergraduate and graduate students only. Advanced registration required.  About Dr. Janice Fine Janice Fine holds a Ph.D. from MIT in Political Science and is a Professor in the Department of Labor Studies and Employment Relations and Director of the workplace justice lab@RU within the School of Management and Labor Relations, Rutgers University where she writes and teaches about economic justice movements and organizations including unions, worker centers, community organizing groups and other forms of collective action in the U.S and cross-nationally; historical and contemporary debates within labor movements regarding immigration; labor standards enforcement; privatization and state capacity for contract oversight. Prior to joining the faculty at Rutgers in 2005, she worked as a labor, community and political organizer and trainer for over twenty years and continues to collaborate with unions, worker centers, immigrant rights organizations and community organizing groups. Fine leads the Labor Standards Enforcement and Base-building projects. She is also a member of the graduate faculty in Political Science and the Department of Latino and Hispanic Caribbean Studies at Rutgers.
name: RU Organizing: An Introduction to the Building Blocks of Organizing for Change

description: A Pitch Perfect Showdown is a music-centered GBM inspired by the iconic riff-off scene from Pitch Perfect. The event brings together short, fast-paced musical rounds where individuals or groups jump in with brief song clips that match specific themes, creating a high-energy, collaborative atmosphere rather than a traditional karaoke setup.   Designed to be fun, welcoming, and low-pressure, this GBM focuses on celebrating Black and Queer joy through shared music and crowd energy. Participants are encouraged to sing solo, perform in groups, or pass the mic freely, while audience members play an active role by hyping performers and voting through applause. With themed rounds, prizes, and snacks, the event creates an engaging space for connection, creativity, and community-building.
name: PITCH PLEASE!  - BBQ Riff-Off: A Pitch Perfect Showdown

description: Join us for another installment of our SSPAR talk series, where Spencer Stubbs will be presenting. For dinner, we have some delicious pizzas and sides from Sizzle n' Slice! Please RSVP so we have a good idea of how much food to order. Title: What is Spin? Abstract: Spin is one of the first examples of a quantum system that most physics students are taught about. From a formal perspective this is fine; it is an abstract quantity with a finite Hilbert space which is useful for teaching the properties of quantum mechanics to students. However, an issue arises when the students are told that fundamental particles have a spin degree of freedom. Naturally students want to know how to think about this quantity, but they are told that it is improper to think of it as a literal spinning ball and to simply accept that particle spin is an inherently quantum property with no classical analog. This talk hopes to demonstrate, in a pedagogical manner, that this is not entirely true and that there is in fact a way that one can attach a classical origin to the idea of spin.
location: W330, Department of Physics and Astronomy
name: SSPAR (Student Seminars in Physics and Astronomy at Rutgers) - "What is Spin?"

We also think that having this event during GAYpril would be very meaningful as well. Having these conversations and creating these spaces on campus are incredibly important when discussing intersectionality. Our goal is to advocate for Afro-Latinx students on campus, and we believe that having this program would allow students to come together and discuss important issues.

description: This co-organized event between Rutgers and Princeton University marks the inaugural meeting of scholars from both institutions focusing on Abya Yala, the Kuna term for the indigenous territory that encompasses what is now known as Latin America.  Taking up the notion of “trans-indigenous” methodologies as proposed by Chadwick Allen – that is, thinking about the indigenous beyond the imposition of colonial borders– our gathering explores connections across indigenous communities throughout the Americas, and invites scholars from across the region to participate in this conversation.  With an interdisciplinary focus, the event brings together topics such as indigenous literature, film, ancestral medicinal practices, and oral testimony to examine how indigenous knowledge systems and traditions circulate and resonate across borders, languages, and communities. We will be joined by guest keynote speaker, poet and professor Odi González (NYU), who will address the complex process of translating oral Quechua literature into written languages, such as Spanish and English. The event will finish with a film screening of Javier Corcuera's Sigo siendo (2013), a documentary which dives into the heterogenous musical expressions of Peru, influenced by local cultures and repertoires from both indigenous and Afro-Peruvian roots. With a focus on migrant subjects in the industrial capital, the film highlights the impossibility of conceiving contemporary indigenous societies in isolation, celebrating the diverse community ties they create as they work to maintain their cultural vibrancy.  Trans-Indigenous Abiayala  1st Dialogue in Indigenous Studies at Rutgers-Princeton University  April 10th Presentations 2:00 a 3:45 pm   Oriele Benavides, Princeton - "Un brusco deseo de volver”: Ficciones contrafundacionales en Piñén y El territorio del viaje de Daniela Catrileo" Gastón Pérez, Princeton - “Vectorizar el Mapudungun: peligros, oportunidades y soberanía en las inteligencias artificiales indígenas” Isabel Hackett, Princeton - From José María Arguedas to the Peruvian Truth Commission: An Acoustic Paradigm for Indigenous Rights  Estela Imigo, Princeton - Sanación, imagen y palabra: articulaciones entre el cine medicina y literatura mapuche en la obra de Francisco Huichaqueo Paul Montjoy Forti, Rutgers - “Humanidades aplicadas: Creación del Consejo Intercultural Indígena de Sabiduría Ancestral para la Conservación de los Microbiomas y la Salud- CISAM SALUD” María Chacón Rendon, Rutgers - “La cuestión indígena en la prensa nacionalista boliviana pos-Chaco: La Calle y la interpretación del indio.”    Coffee Break: Café 3:45 a 4:00   Main speaker talk  4:00 a 5:00    Odi Gonzales, NYU Professor  Translation of Oral Languages and Cultures: The Collision Between Two Worlds.  Film exhibition 5:30 pm Sigo siendo (Kackaniraqmi) (2013) by Javier Corcuera Cierre 7:30 pm
name: Trans-Indigenous Abya Yala  1st Dialogue in Indigenous Studies at Rutgers-Princeton University
organizationName: Literary Studies in Spanish Organization (Graduate Organization)

description: Please join SPCR for the workshop "The Things We Hold" to explore the experiences of working mothers, the multitasking, the invisible labor, and the things we carry in our daily lives. This workshop will be kid-friendly, and participants will create a bag and fill it with the things they carry. Refreshments and beverages are also provided! *Spaces are limited to 12 families (12 RSVPs). Please RSVP to register! “The Things We Hold” is a creative, reflective workshop that invites mothers and children to explore what they carry in their lives, both tangible and invisible. Inspired by Ursula K. Le Guin’s idea that the first human tool might have been a bag, participants create personalized “carrier bags” filled with objects, memories, feelings, responsibilities, dreams, and routines. Through drawing, writing, decorating, and collaborative conversation, families reflect on what sustains, burdens, or delights them. The process encourages curiosity, empathy, and dialogue, prompting questions like “Who carries this more?” or “Is this heavy or light?” The workshop concludes with a sharing circle and portrait documentation, offering space to acknowledge both the visible and invisible parts of our daily lives, and to consider what we might wish to carry less of.
name: SPCR Presents: “The Things We Hold” Workshop
organizationName: Supporting Parents and Caregivers at Rutgers (Graduate Organization)
organizationNames: Graduate Student Life (Department at Rutgers University)
Supporting Parents and Caregivers at Rutgers (Graduate Organization)

Each session offers guided prompts as well as the freedom to create in your own style and medium—whether drawing, painting, writing, or any other form of artistic expression. This group encourages open dialogue, reflection, and connection while using art as a tool for self-discovery and communication.

description: Sam Schwartz Transportation Research Program: The Inaugural Future of Transportation Conference — Autonomous Vehicles: the Benefits, the Risks, and the Need for Regulation    When?       Thursday, April 16, 2026, 2:15 pm to 5:30 pm       Where?       Roosevelt House @ Hunter College (Upper East Side, NYC) - also virtual option       How to get there?       Take NJT to Penn Station, walk to Herald Square on 6th Ave, and take the N/Q/R/W to 5th Ave / 59 St or Lexington Ave / 63rd stations.       How to register?       https://host.nxt.blackbaud.com/registration-form/?formId=78405bda-b596-448e-95c2-660a68f479ab&envId=p-rzKFBb2xVk253Ulcjd_x1w&zone=usa (in-person or virtual)       How much?       Free!       Reimbursement?       Free ticket, but we will apply for travel / dinner funding!       Why?      (copied from Artem): “Hi everyone, Roosevelt House and Sam Schwartz (the guy who coined term gridlock and former NYC transportation commissioner and consultant) are doing this transportation conference focused on AVs, thought it might be of interest to someone”     New York Transit Museum - Interborough Express MTA talk    When?       Thursday, April 16, 2026, 6:00 pm to 7:30 pm (time for chatting with presenters and other attendees afterwards)       Where?       New York Transit Museum       How to get there?       The best way from New Brunswick is usually taking NJT to Penn Station and taking the A/C to Jay St-Metrotech. We can go together from New Brunswick!       How to register?     Get your $15 ticket here       How much?       Member tickets are $10; if you want to get your own it’s $15. NJT plus subway round trip is $39.10.       Reimbursement?       Yes, as long as GSA approves the request! Save your NJT / MTA tickets and receipts, and your ticket email and proof of payment (if you got your own).       Why?      Transit Museum talks are usually short but informative! A lot of transit nerds and professionals (especially MTA) attend from the NYC area, and the presenters usually stick around to answer questions after. Plus, the IBX is a cool project and I’ve heard that the people working on it are very receptive to hearing new ideas.
name: Hunter College Autonomous Vehicles Conference / New York Transit Museum Interborough Express
organizationName: Bloustein Enthusiasts and Advocates for Transportation

description: ECE-GSA DOUBLES TABLE TENNIS TOURNAMENT Think you've got game? The ECE-GSA is hosting a doubles table tennis tournament on April 15-16 – two days of competitive action right here on the 5th floor of CoRE. Sign up as a pair, or register solo and we'll match you with a partner.  You can pick the time-slot which is the most convenient for you in the registration form. Final match-ups and time-slots will be shared on the week of April 6th! Space is limited, so grab your partner and lock in your spot early! Register by April 6th. description: Join the GSA Tabletop Gaming Committee at Rutgers Thursday, February 19th7:00 PM Onwards College Avenue Student Center, Room 108 All graduate and undergraduate students are welcome!Graduate school can be intense, which is why the GSA Tabletop Gaming Committee is here to help you unwind, recharge, and connect. description: Want to get more confident speaking in class, working in teams, or handling disagreements without things getting awkward? Join Improv for Democracy — a fun, interactive workshop where you’ll practice real communication skills through improv (no acting experience needed).   🗓 Friday, April 17 | 1:15–2:30 PM📍 Labor Education Center Auditorium🍕 + 🥗 Free lunch available at 12:30 pm REGISTER NOW! Space is limited.   You’ll learn how to: 🎯 Speak up without overthinking🎯 Handle different opinions with confidence🎯 Think on your feet in discussions and group work🎯 Communicate more effectively (great for interviews too) ✨ Great experiential learning activity for resumes and leadership development.   No prep. No pressure. Just show up and participate. Bring a friend — it’ll be fun, practical, and way more interactive than a typical lecture. REGISTER NOW! Space is limited.   Co-sponsored by: School of Management and Labor Relations, The Institute of Inclusive Pedagogy, the Edward J. Bloustein School of Planning and Public Policy, the School of Social Work, the Mason Gross School of Arts, the Graduate School of Education, Rutgers Business School, and the School of Communication and Information. The event is supported by Rutgers Democracy Lab and is a part of the Solving Grand Challenges Month.
name: Improv for Democracy
organizationName: School of Management and Labor Relations
organizationNames: Edward J. Bloustein School of Planning and Public Policy (Department at Rutgers)
School of Management and Labor Relations
Rutgers Graduate School of Education (University Department)

description: The First Annual Conference: Language at Crossroads invites scholars, graduate students, and researchers to contribute to an interdisciplinary dialogue on language across its multiple levels of analysis. The conference will feature four panels: panel 1: phonetics and phonology, panel 2: morphology and syntax, panel 3: semantics and pragmatics, and panel 4: project launch. Each panel is open to contributions engaging with these levels of language, broadly conceived. Thus, contributions to neurolinguistics, cognitive sciences, psychology, psycholinguistics, sociolinguistics, discourse analysis, education, cultural studies, and related fields. Abstract Submission: here by March 20th Register: here by April 3rd
name: 1st Annual Conference: Language at Crossroads

description: Colors of Identity: Paint Your Flag is hosted by the Honors College LGBTQ+ Affinity Group in collaboration with the LGBTQ+ Learning Collaborative and LLEGO. This event offers a creative and welcoming space for students to explore and express their identities by painting a flag that represents who they are, whether that reflects their sexuality, gender identity, cultural background, or personal experiences. The event aims to foster connection, celebrate the diversity within the LGBTQ+ community, and provide an affirming environment where students feel comfortable expressing themselves and engaging with others. Food and drinks will be provided, and there will also be a giveaway featuring copies of Heated Rivalry and various LGBTQ+ flags.
name: Colors of Identity: Paint Your Flag
organizationNames: LLEGO: The LGBTQQIA People of Color Organization at Rutgers

description: Spring is here.  Join the Rutgers Global Student Volunteer Corps for a fun evening of flower making.  Our student leaders will teach attendees how to create beautiful flowers out of paper, pipe cleaners, and other materials.  By the end of the evening, you will leave with a small bouquet of realistic looking flowers to enjoy on your own or share with someone else.
name: Spring Blooms: Craft Night

description: Please join us on April 18, 2026 for the Rutgers Tree Planting Festival, where we will be planting 3000 trees using the Miyawaki Method in place of an abandoned roadway that was removed last year by Rutgers Institutional Planning and Operations. RSVP HERE!! --> https://rutgers.ca1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_41L7gvHGcte3Q2y  The goal of this event is to build community by collectively contributing to the environment and giving students first-hand experience in tree planting. There will be trees, art, and music!  This event is open to all members of the Rutgers community!  Sign up for the 10:00am-12:00pm, 12:00pm-2:00pm, or 2:00pm-5:00pm. (But you can come and go as you please!)
name: RU Tree Planting Festival
organizationNames: Scarlet Arts Rx (Affiliated with a Mason Gross School of the Arts Department)

description: Drop-in for an Artful Saturday monthly event hosted by the Center for the Women in the Arts at Douglass and Scarlet Arts RX! Join us for a Spring painting picnic in Jameson courtyard (rain location: Ludwig Center). Artsy door prizes! Bring a friend and a blanket to sit on (if you have one)! Taught by a Scarlet Arts Rx student teaching artist from Rutgers Mason Gross School of the Arts. Free supplies, snacks, and boba provided! Supplies are limited – First come, first served. All skill levels and walk-ins welcome! Foundational art skills you will learn at this event: Painting and referencing nature, Color theory and color relationships. This experience counts towards the Douglass Discovery curriculum requirement. R3: Career/Discovery/Professional Development/Research.Reminder: To receive DRC curriculum credit, you must complete a reflection survey. ___ * Image and Media Release By attending this event, you acknowledge and agree that Rutgers University may photograph, record, or otherwise capture your artwork, image, and/or voice. You grant Rutgers the unrestricted right to use, reproduce, publish, and distribute these sound, still, or moving images in any medium for educational, promotional, advertising, or other purposes aligned with the University’s mission. All rights to these materials will belong to Rutgers University. Please let our photographer or videographer know if you do not want your image taken at the event.
location: Jameson Courtyard / Schonberg Rm, Ludwig (rain location)

description: Come join us on Sunday, April 19, for Haru's biggest event of the semester, Spring 2026 Showcase! Our goal is to share Korean pop culture through the art of dance. This event is open to everyone: general members, families, Rutgers students, and non-Rutgers students! Our showcase is our biggest event, where our members get to showcase a multitude of K-pop performances they’ve been preparing for months for our audience. There are two acts with an intermission, which includes a raffle with a bunch of prizes our audience can partake in and possibly win big! And the best part is that admission is FREE for all!  You don't have to be performing to attend, so come out and support our performers! ♡ Let's cheer each other on and enjoy each other's company! You may also get some sweet treats, snacks, and refreshments from us to enjoy while watching performances 👀 Our showcase is also livestreamed on our YouTube page for audience members who cannot attend in person, but would love to support our performers! If you're watching from online, thank you for supporting Haru Dance ♡ More information on parking will be detailed here later in the semester. Contact us directly on IG: @harudance_official or Email: haru.kdcc@gmail.com if you have any questions! Can't wait to see y'all soon!
location: Cook/Douglass - Trayes Hall Douglass Student Center
name: HARU Spring 2026 Showcase

description: CityNerd Live at Hunter College    When?       Friday, April 24, 2026, 6:00 pm to 9:00 pm       Where?       Hunter College Assembly Hall       How to get there?       The best way from New Brunswick is usually taking NJT to Penn Station and taking the E to the 6. Should take roughly 1.5 hours.       How to register?       Get your $20 ticket here       How much?       Tickets are $20. Travel is $33.10 round trip on NJT and $6.00 round trip on the subway.       Reimbursement?       Yes, as long as GSA approves the request! Save your NJT / MTA tickets and receipts, and your ticket email and proof of payment (if you got your own).       Why?      CityNerd makes entertaining videos (at least imo), and I’m sure it will be a fun discussion. Betsy Plum (Riders Alliance) and Tiffany-Ann Taylor (Regional Plan Association) are also there.
name: CityNerd Live at Hunter College
organizationName: Bloustein Enthusiasts and Advocates for Transportation

description: 2026 North Jersey Transit Forum    What?       Join the North Jersey Transit Forum and engage in meaningful discussions on urban planning and transportation policies. This event brings together stakeholders, transportation agencies, government, nonprofits, educators and the business community to develop a common vision for the future of transit and mobility in North Jersey and the Tri-State region.       When?       Saturday, April 25, 2026, 8:00 am to 6:00 pm  7:15am     Doors open, Registration & Breakfast 8:00am   Keynote, talks & panels 12:30pm   Lunch 1:45pm   Infrastructure Tours Depart 4:30pm  Afternoon Tea & Coffee 5:00pm   Closing Remarks 6:00pm   Happy Hour        Where?       Mac Mahon Student Center - The Duncan Family Sky Room - Saint Peter's University - 47 Glenwood Ave, Jersey City, NJ 07306       How to get there?       Congregate at New Brunswick station at 6:20 AM, leave on the 6:27 AM NEC to NY Penn, change to PATH, arrive at Journal Square around 7:45 AM.       How to register?       Get your $45 student ticket here       Reimbursement?       Yes, as long as GSA approves the request! Save your NJT / PATH tickets and receipts, and your ticket email and proof of payment (if you got your own).       Why?      Many high-up politicians, transit professionals, and advocates will be present. Talks will include first/last-mile transit, capital programs, better PATH and light rail, and BRT. Sounds like a lot of educational and networking opportunities!
location: Mac Mahon Student Center - The Duncan Family Sky Room - Saint Peter's University
name: 2026 North Jersey Transit Forum
organizationName: Bloustein Enthusiasts and Advocates for Transportation

Study in the galleries and enjoy free provided food and refreshments in our lobby as well as a variety of brain break activities! Rutgers student ID required. Visit event page for all details & updates.
name: Study Break for Rutgers Students

description: The 2026 Graduate Research in Interdisciplinary Biosciences (GRIB) Conference aims to understand how modern environments interact with natural biological systems, and how these interactions shape metabolic health, neuropsychiatric function, and overall human well-being. On May 6, 2026, GRIB will convene at ARH-200 on the Cook/Douglass campus, bringing together perspectives from the biological, psychological, and social sciences. The conference will:  Dr. Vogel will examine humanity’s evolutionary foundations, with emphasis on the environmental and metabolic conditions that shaped human biology. Dr. LeDoux will investigate the neural basis of emotion and subjective experience, and how modern contexts influence and at times disrupt these processes. Dena Seidel will explore pathways toward greater alignment between individuals, societies, and their environments, highlighting opportunities for better communicating innovation in health, culture, and design.  Participants are invited to engage with a shared aim: to understand how scientific progress can support health span in populations and foster lives that are more coherent, adaptive, and meaningful, with a greater sense of integration within the modern landscape.
name: Graduate Research in Interdisciplinary Biosciences (GRIB) Conference - Relationship Between the Modern Environment and Human Biology
organizationNames: Department of Plant Biology (Cook Researchers of Plant Science) (Graduate Organization)

description: Come celebrate the joy of singing with this day-long event of reconnecting with old friends and meeting new friends! If you have ever sung in a Rutgers choir at any point in your journey at Rutgers, join us on this day of singing. There will be light refreshments in the morning, and then lunch is on your own. We end the day with a relaxed performance of the pieces we have worked on throughout the day.
